title: Image Resolution & Dimensions
concept: An image's size is described as width x height x channels, and because every pixel and channel is a number to process, higher resolution means dramatically more data and computation.

exercises.prompt: A photo is described as '1920 x 1080'. What do you think those two numbers mean?

options.text: The width and height of the image in pixels
The file's release year and its price
The number of colors and the file size in bytes

exercises.explain: Right! Those numbers are the image's width and height, measured in pixels.

cards.title: Why does a 'higher quality' photo also mean a bigger file?
cards.body: You've probably noticed that high-resolution photos take longer to upload and eat up more phone storage than blurry, low-res ones. That's not a coincidence — resolution directly controls how many numbers make up the image, and more numbers means more storage and more work for any program that processes it.

cards.title: Width, height, and channels together define an image's size
cards.body: An image's dimensions are usually written as width × height × channels — for example, 1920 × 1080 × 3 for a full-HD color photo. Width and height say how many pixels wide and tall the grid is; channels say how many numbers each pixel holds (1 for grayscale, 3 for RGB). Multiply all three together and you get the total count of numbers in the image.

cards.title: Like counting bricks in a wall, not just measuring it
cards.body: A wall described as '10 bricks wide by 5 bricks tall' has 50 bricks total — simple multiplication. An image works the same way: more pixels wide and more pixels tall means more total pixels, and each pixel might need multiple numbers (channels) just like a fancy brick might need multiple layers of paint.

cards.title: Counting the numbers in a real photo
cards.body: A modest 100 × 100 pixel color photo has 100 × 100 × 3 = 30,000 numbers. Bump the resolution up to a common phone photo size, 4000 × 3000, and it jumps to 4000 × 3000 × 3 = 36,000,000 numbers — over a thousand times more data, just from increasing the resolution.

cards.title: 'Higher resolution' isn't automatically 'better'
cards.body: It's tempting to think more pixels is always the goal. But higher resolution means more storage, slower processing, and sometimes no visible improvement if the task doesn't need that much detail. Many practical systems deliberately shrink images down before processing them, trading a bit of detail for a lot of speed.

cards.title: Small image vs. large image, same scene
cards.body: More pixels always means more numbers to store and process.

left.text:
Fewer pixels, less detail.
Fewer numbers to store and process.
Faster, lighter, sometimes blurry.

right.text:
Many more pixels, sharp detail.
Millions more numbers to store and process.
Slower, heavier, more storage needed.

exercises.prompt: In the notation 'width × height × channels', what does the 'channels' number represent?

options.text: How many values each pixel stores (like 1 for grayscale, 3 for RGB)
The number of pixels in the whole image

exercises.explain: Correct. Channels count how many numbers are stored per pixel.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Doubling both the width and height of an image roughly quadruples its total pixel count.
exercises.explain: True. Doubling width AND height multiplies pixel count by 2 x 2 = 4.

exercises.sentence: A color photo's total size in numbers is width times height times ___.
exercises.explain: Channels is correct. Multiplying width, height, and channels gives the total pixel-value count.

exercises.prompt: Which statement about resolution shows correct understanding?

options.text: A higher-resolution image has more total numbers to store and process than a lower-resolution one of the same scene
Resolution only affects file color, not size or computation
All images, regardless of width and height, contain the same number of pixels

exercises.explain: Exactly. More width and height pixels directly means more data and more work for any program handling it.

exercises.prompt: A phone app needs to process photos quickly on an older device with limited processing power. The photos come in at 4000 x 3000 x 3. What's the most sensible move before processing?

options.text: Shrink the image to a smaller resolution first, trading some detail for much less data to process
Always process the image at full resolution, since more data is always better
Add more color channels to make processing faster
Resolution has no effect on processing time, so do nothing

exercises.explain: Exactly. Reducing resolution cuts the number of pixel values dramatically, which is a common practical trade-off for speed.
